question: [File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Rounding, fixed-point math, mulDiv, fee, spread, or time-to-maturity error that creates extractable value or systematic value leakage'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take (settlement fee with TTM between 180 and 360 days, large cbp difference)] Can an unprivileged taker, under preconditions where settlementFeeCbp5 (180d) = 250 and settlementFeeCbp6 (360d) = 500 (maximum allowed values), take an offer at TTM = 270 days (midpoint) and pay a fee of 375 cbp, and does the linear interpolation produce exactly 375 cbp or does integer division truncate to 374 cbp, creating a 1-cbp fee underpayment per trade? Proof idea: set cbp5=250, cbp6=500, assert settlementFee(id, 270 days) == 375 * CBP or 374 * CBP, and determine which rounding direction is used.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Rounding, fixed-point math, mulDiv, fee, spread, or time-to-maturity error that creates extractable value or systematic value leakage'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take (TickLib.priceToTick rounding up to nearest spacing multiple)] Can an unprivileged taker, given an offer at tick T where T is a multiple of tickSpacing, exploit the fact that priceToTick rounds up to the nearest spacing multiple, causing the effective price used in the take to be higher than tickToPrice(T), and does this rounding create a systematic advantage for the taker or maker? Proof idea: for each tick T that is a multiple of DEFAULT_TICK_SPACING=4, assert tickToPrice(priceToTick(tickToPrice(T), 4)) == tickToPrice(T) (round-trip consistency).
